title: Pyelonephritis — Acute
oneLiner: Acute bacterial kidney infection; common in young women. Sepsis, obstruction, or abscess will kill if missed in first hour.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Septic shock:** single finding = lactate >2 mmol/L **or** NEWS2 ≥5 despite 30 mL/kg IV fluids (not just a raised CRP)
- **Infected obstructed kidney (calculus / papillary necrosis):** single finding = severe flank pain + anuria/oliguria + fever – this needs **urgent urology referral** for decompression (retrograde stent or nephrostomy) before any antibiotic will work
- **Perinephric or renal abscess:** single finding = spiking fevers >72 hours despite appropriate IV antibiotics and no clinical improvement
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms (3–5):**
  - Fever (often >38.5°C) with rigors
  - Unilateral loin/flank pain (dull ache, may radiate to lower abdomen/groin)
  - Dysuria, frequency, urgency (often present but not dominant)
  - Nausea/vomiting (common, can lead to dehydration)
  - Malaise, myalgia
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Costovertebral angle tenderness (CVA percussion – posterior approach)
  - Temperature >38°C, tachycardia
  - Suprapubic tenderness only if concurrent cystitis
  - Not usually abdo rigidity/rebound – if present, consider peritonitis (stone perforation, duodenal ulcer)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Cystitis (lower UTI):** flank pain absent, CVA negative, no fever/rigors
- **Renal/ureteric calculus:** colicky pain radiating to groin, microscopic haematuria, little to no fever. If fever + stone → infected obstructed kidney (emergency)
- **Renal abscess:** prolonged fever despite abx, may have a palpable mass, WCC very high, CT shows fluid collection
- **Pancreatitis:** epigastric pain radiating to back, vomiting prominent, lipase elevated; no CVA tenderness, no urinary nitrites
- **Pneumonia (lower lobe):** cough, pleuritic chest pain, chest signs; urine dip negative
- **Pyelonephritis vs. stone: splitter:** Flank pain **with** fever → pyelo until proven otherwise; flank pain **without** fever → stone first
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Urine dipstick: nitrites (specific but ~50% sensitive), leucocytes, blood
  - Urine pregnancy test (all women of childbearing age)
  - ECG only if chest pain or syncope (not routine)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (neutrophilia), U&E (baseline renal function for antibiotic dosing and AKI risk), CRP, lactate
  - Blood cultures **before** first antibiotic dose (two sets from separate sites)
  - Consider blood glucose (diabetes is a risk factor for severe disease)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Renal ultrasound within 24 hours if complicated – i.e. previous stones, pregnancy, diabetes, immunosuppression, recurrent pyelo, male, or no response to abx
  - **Second-line:** CT KUB (non-contrast) if suspicion of stone or obstruction on USS; CT with contrast if abscess suspected
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** – determines antibiotic choice (avoid tetracyclines, fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides if pregnant or breastfeeding). Also **blood cultures** before antibiotics – without them, you lose microbiological diagnosis and can’t rationalise therapy later.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess NEWS2, lactate; if ≥5 or lactate >2 → start sepsis 6 bundle (give 30 mL/kg crystalloid, IV antibiotics within 1 hour, blood cultures, lactate, urine output monitoring, consider escalation)
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1 g IV/PO; if mild/moderate and no contraindication (eGFR >30, no CI to NSAIDs) add ibuprofen 400 mg PO or diclofenac IM/PR
  - Antiemetic: metoclopramide 10 mg IV/IM or ondansetron 4 mg IV/Oral (avoid prolonged QT)
  - Start IV antibiotics (see Drugs section below)
- **Ward (first 24–72 hours):**
  - Continue IV antibiotics for 48–72 hours; then switch to oral if afebrile for 24–48 hours, clinical improvement, and able to tolerate oral
  - Monitor urine output, daily U&E (AKI common in elderly/sepsis), repeat CRP day 2–3
  - If no improvement by 48 hours → repeat blood cultures, urine culture, and arrange urgent renal USS/CT
  - Ensure adequate fluid intake (oral or IV) to maintain urine output >0.5 mL/kg/hr
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete 7–14 days total antibiotic course (IV + oral). Uncomplicated pyelo in women: 7–10 days; complicated/male/pregnant: 10–14 days
  - Review urine culture results and adjust oral antibiotic accordingly
  - If recurrent pyelo (>2 in 6 months) → consider renal ultrasound, cystoscopy, and nephrology referral
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride at 125 mL/hour (adjust for AKI, fluid overload, or sepsis ongoing)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (if eGFR >30) or [check local policy for low weight/eGFR]
  - **Diet/NBM:** Free fluids as tolerated; NBM only if vomiting or planned for intervention
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS, urine output chart (consider catheter only if shocked or unable to void)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line IV (mild-moderate):**
  - Co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV 8-hourly (if no penicillin allergy)
  - If penicillin allergic: Cefuroxime 750 mg–1.5 g IV 8-hourly (except anaphylaxis) OR Ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV 12-hourly
- **First-line IV (severe sepsis/septic shock):**
  - Broad-spectrum per local sepsis protocol: e.g. Piperacillin-tazobactam (Tazocin) 4.5 g IV 6-hourly OR Ceftriaxone 2 g IV once daily (if no penicillin allergy and not MRSA/ESBL risk) **plus** Gentamicin (single dose 5–7 mg/kg IV) [check local policy for gent dosing]
- **Oral step-down (after clinical improvement and culture available):**
  - Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO twice daily (avoid if pregnant <18 years, or if tendonopathy history). Preferred due to high renal tissue penetration
  - Or Co-amoxiclav 625 mg PO 8-hourly (if sensitive)
  - Or Trimethoprim 200 mg PO twice daily (if sensitive)
  - Or Nitrofurantoin MR 100 mg PO twice daily (only for cystitis, not pyelo – poor renal tissue penetration)
- **Key contraindications:**
  - Fluoroquinolones: pregnancy, <18 years, known QT prolongation, myasthenia gravis, tendon rupture history
  - Gentamicin: pregnancy, known auditory/vestibular problems; avoid in myasthenia gravis
  - Co-amoxiclav: penicillin anaphylaxis, prior cholestatic jaundice
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Gentamicin:** Calculate dose using ideal body weight; check trough/peak levels if >48 hours of therapy; reduce dose in eGFR <30 (use once-daily but extended interval). Most dangerous dose error: giving a second dose without checking levels (accumulates, causes ototoxicity/nephrotoxicity)
  - **Ciprofloxacin:** Dose halved for eGFR 15–29; avoid if eGFR <15 or dialysis
  - **Co-amoxiclav:** Caution in severe hepatic impairment (avoid if previous jaundice); dose adjust for eGFR <30 (e.g. 625 mg PO BD if eGFR <30)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Gentamicin – not checking renal function pre-dose and not monitoring levels** (ototoxicity/nephrotoxicity). **Ciprofloxacin – in elderly, concomitant NSAIDs and steroid → increased CNS excitability (seizures, confusion).** **Co-amoxiclav – mistaken use of high-dose IV in renal impairment without adjustment → increased risk of neurotoxicity/platelet dysfunction.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Septic shock** (leading cause of death in acute pyelo)
  - **Acute kidney injury** (especially in elderly, volume depleted, septic)
  - **Renal/perinephric abscess**
  - **Urosepsis with multiorgan failure**
  - **Infected obstructive uropathy** (stone, papillary necrosis, malignant obstruction) → emergency decompression
- **Chronic:**
  - **Renal scarring** leading to hypertension, chronic kidney disease (especially if recurrent)
  - **Recurrent pyelo** (risk of further structural damage)
  - **Emphysematous pyelonephritis** (gas-forming infection, high mortality, frequent in diabetics – urgent nephrectomy often needed)
sections.Prognosis:
- Uncomplicated pyelo in young women → >95% recover fully with 7–10 days of appropriate abx.
- Complicated (elderly, CKD, diabetes, obstruction) → higher morbidity, mortality ~5–10% if sepsis develops.
- Emphysematous pyelonephritis → mortality up to 40% if not surgically treated.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always do a pregnancy test** before starting antibiotics in any woman of childbearing age – it changes your choice of abx and your referral (to obstetric medicine).
- **Obtain blood cultures before first antibiotic dose** – this is the only way to get a microbiological diagnosis if the urine culture is contaminated or the patient has already taken oral antibiotics.
- **If the patient fails to improve after 48 hours of IV antibiotics, repeat blood cultures and check for obstruction – order a renal ultrasound within 24 hours.**
- **Use ciprofloxacin for oral step-down** if susceptible – it achieves high renal tissue levels and shortens hospital stay.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT use nitrofurantoin for pyelonephritis** – it does not concentrate in renal parenchyma; it is only for cystitis.
- **Do NOT give gentamicin without checking baseline creatinine and then monitoring levels** – this is a serious error that can cause permanent hearing loss or acute kidney injury.
- **Do NOT delay imaging in a septic patient with severe flank pain** – an obstructed kidney requires urgent decompression; antibiotics alone are ineffective.
- **Do NOT rely solely on urine dipstick** – negative nitrites do not rule out pyelo (false negatives common in enterococcal infections); always send a culture.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** High fever with rigors that does not settle, severe flank pain unresponsive to oral analgesia, inability to keep down fluids (vomiting), or dark/reduced urine output.
- **Follow-up:** If uncomplicated, no routine follow-up needed unless symptoms recur. If complicated (pregnancy, diabetes, CKD, recurrent), arrange GP review at 48–72 hours to check urine culture result, and consider outpatient renology/urology referral.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially if increasing despite fluids)
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L on repeat
  - Oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/hr for 2 hours)
  - Suspected obstruction (anuria, severe colicky pain)
  - No clinical response to IV antibiotics after 48 hours
- **Consider ICU referral if:**
  - Need for vasopressors (falling BP despite 30 mL/kg IV fluids)
  - GCS <15 or confusion
  - Respiratory rate >25 or SpO2 <92% on O2
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - Acute renal replacement therapy indicated (e.g. refractory hyperkalaemia, fluid overload, uraemia)

rawMarkdown:
## Pyelonephritis — Acute

**One-liner:** Acute bacterial kidney infection; common in young women. Sepsis, obstruction, or abscess will kill if missed in first hour.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Septic shock:** single finding = lactate >2 mmol/L **or** NEWS2 ≥5 despite 30 mL/kg IV fluids (not just a raised CRP)
- **Infected obstructed kidney (calculus / papillary necrosis):** single finding = severe flank pain + anuria/oliguria + fever – this needs **urgent urology referral** for decompression (retrograde stent or nephrostomy) before any antibiotic will work
- **Perinephric or renal abscess:** single finding = spiking fevers >72 hours despite appropriate IV antibiotics and no clinical improvement

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms (3–5):**
  - Fever (often >38.5°C) with rigors
  - Unilateral loin/flank pain (dull ache, may radiate to lower abdomen/groin)
  - Dysuria, frequency, urgency (often present but not dominant)
  - Nausea/vomiting (common, can lead to dehydration)
  - Malaise, myalgia
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Costovertebral angle tenderness (CVA percussion – posterior approach)
  - Temperature >38°C, tachycardia
  - Suprapubic tenderness only if concurrent cystitis
  - Not usually abdo rigidity/rebound – if present, consider peritonitis (stone perforation, duodenal ulcer)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Cystitis (lower UTI):** flank pain absent, CVA negative, no fever/rigors
- **Renal/ureteric calculus:** colicky pain radiating to groin, microscopic haematuria, little to no fever. If fever + stone → infected obstructed kidney (emergency)
- **Renal abscess:** prolonged fever despite abx, may have a palpable mass, WCC very high, CT shows fluid collection
- **Pancreatitis:** epigastric pain radiating to back, vomiting prominent, lipase elevated; no CVA tenderness, no urinary nitrites
- **Pneumonia (lower lobe):** cough, pleuritic chest pain, chest signs; urine dip negative
- **Pyelonephritis vs. stone: splitter:** Flank pain **with** fever → pyelo until proven otherwise; flank pain **without** fever → stone first

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Urine dipstick: nitrites (specific but ~50% sensitive), leucocytes, blood
  - Urine pregnancy test (all women of childbearing age)
  - ECG only if chest pain or syncope (not routine)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (neutrophilia), U&E (baseline renal function for antibiotic dosing and AKI risk), CRP, lactate
  - Blood cultures **before** first antibiotic dose (two sets from separate sites)
  - Consider blood glucose (diabetes is a risk factor for severe disease)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Renal ultrasound within 24 hours if complicated – i.e. previous stones, pregnancy, diabetes, immunosuppression, recurrent pyelo, male, or no response to abx
  - **Second-line:** CT KUB (non-contrast) if suspicion of stone or obstruction on USS; CT with contrast if abscess suspected
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** – determines antibiotic choice (avoid tetracyclines, fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides if pregnant or breastfeeding). Also **blood cultures** before antibiotics – without them, you lose microbiological diagnosis and can’t rationalise therapy later.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess NEWS2, lactate; if ≥5 or lactate >2 → start sepsis 6 bundle (give 30 mL/kg crystalloid, IV antibiotics within 1 hour, blood cultures, lactate, urine output monitoring, consider escalation)
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1 g IV/PO; if mild/moderate and no contraindication (eGFR >30, no CI to NSAIDs) add ibuprofen 400 mg PO or diclofenac IM/PR
  - Antiemetic: metoclopramide 10 mg IV/IM or ondansetron 4 mg IV/Oral (avoid prolonged QT)
  - Start IV antibiotics (see Drugs section below)
- **Ward (first 24–72 hours):**
  - Continue IV antibiotics for 48–72 hours; then switch to oral if afebrile for 24–48 hours, clinical improvement, and able to tolerate oral
  - Monitor urine output, daily U&E (AKI common in elderly/sepsis), repeat CRP day 2–3
  - If no improvement by 48 hours → repeat blood cultures, urine culture, and arrange urgent renal USS/CT
  - Ensure adequate fluid intake (oral or IV) to maintain urine output >0.5 mL/kg/hr
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete 7–14 days total antibiotic course (IV + oral). Uncomplicated pyelo in women: 7–10 days; complicated/male/pregnant: 10–14 days
  - Review urine culture results and adjust oral antibiotic accordingly
  - If recurrent pyelo (>2 in 6 months) → consider renal ultrasound, cystoscopy, and nephrology referral
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride at 125 mL/hour (adjust for AKI, fluid overload, or sepsis ongoing)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC once daily (if eGFR >30) or [check local policy for low weight/eGFR]
  - **Diet/NBM:** Free fluids as tolerated; NBM only if vomiting or planned for intervention
  - **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS, urine output chart (consider catheter only if shocked or unable to void)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line IV (mild-moderate):**
  - Co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV 8-hourly (if no penicillin allergy)
  - If penicillin allergic: Cefuroxime 750 mg–1.5 g IV 8-hourly (except anaphylaxis) OR Ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV 12-hourly
- **First-line IV (severe sepsis/septic shock):**
  - Broad-spectrum per local sepsis protocol: e.g. Piperacillin-tazobactam (Tazocin) 4.5 g IV 6-hourly OR Ceftriaxone 2 g IV once daily (if no penicillin allergy and not MRSA/ESBL risk) **plus** Gentamicin (single dose 5–7 mg/kg IV) [check local policy for gent dosing]
- **Oral step-down (after clinical improvement and culture available):**
  - Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO twice daily (avoid if pregnant <18 years, or if tendonopathy history). Preferred due to high renal tissue penetration
  - Or Co-amoxiclav 625 mg PO 8-hourly (if sensitive)
  - Or Trimethoprim 200 mg PO twice daily (if sensitive)
  - Or Nitrofurantoin MR 100 mg PO twice daily (only for cystitis, not pyelo – poor renal tissue penetration)
- **Key contraindications:**
  - Fluoroquinolones: pregnancy, <18 years, known QT prolongation, myasthenia gravis, tendon rupture history
  - Gentamicin: pregnancy, known auditory/vestibular problems; avoid in myasthenia gravis
  - Co-amoxiclav: penicillin anaphylaxis, prior cholestatic jaundice
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Gentamicin:** Calculate dose using ideal body weight; check trough/peak levels if >48 hours of therapy; reduce dose in eGFR <30 (use once-daily but extended interval). Most dangerous dose error: giving a second dose without checking levels (accumulates, causes ototoxicity/nephrotoxicity)
  - **Ciprofloxacin:** Dose halved for eGFR 15–29; avoid if eGFR <15 or dialysis
  - **Co-amoxiclav:** Caution in severe hepatic impairment (avoid if previous jaundice); dose adjust for eGFR <30 (e.g. 625 mg PO BD if eGFR <30)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Gentamicin – not checking renal function pre-dose and not monitoring levels** (ototoxicity/nephrotoxicity). **Ciprofloxacin – in elderly, concomitant NSAIDs and steroid → increased CNS excitability (seizures, confusion).** **Co-amoxiclav – mistaken use of high-dose IV in renal impairment without adjustment → increased risk of neurotoxicity/platelet dysfunction.**

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Septic shock** (leading cause of death in acute pyelo)
  - **Acute kidney injury** (especially in elderly, volume depleted, septic)
  - **Renal/perinephric abscess**
  - **Urosepsis with multiorgan failure**
  - **Infected obstructive uropathy** (stone, papillary necrosis, malignant obstruction) → emergency decompression
- **Chronic:**
  - **Renal scarring** leading to hypertension, chronic kidney disease (especially if recurrent)
  - **Recurrent pyelo** (risk of further structural damage)
  - **Emphysematous pyelonephritis** (gas-forming infection, high mortality, frequent in diabetics – urgent nephrectomy often needed)

### Prognosis
- Uncomplicated pyelo in young women → >95% recover fully with 7–10 days of appropriate abx.
- Complicated (elderly, CKD, diabetes, obstruction) → higher morbidity, mortality ~5–10% if sepsis develops.
- Emphysematous pyelonephritis → mortality up to 40% if not surgically treated.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always do a pregnancy test** before starting antibiotics in any woman of childbearing age – it changes your choice of abx and your referral (to obstetric medicine).
- **Obtain blood cultures before first antibiotic dose** – this is the only way to get a microbiological diagnosis if the urine culture is contaminated or the patient has already taken oral antibiotics.
- **If the patient fails to improve after 48 hours of IV antibiotics, repeat blood cultures and check for obstruction – order a renal ultrasound within 24 hours.**
- **Use ciprofloxacin for oral step-down** if susceptible – it achieves high renal tissue levels and shortens hospital stay.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT use nitrofurantoin for pyelonephritis** – it does not concentrate in renal parenchyma; it is only for cystitis.
- **Do NOT give gentamicin without checking baseline creatinine and then monitoring levels** – this is a serious error that can cause permanent hearing loss or acute kidney injury.
- **Do NOT delay imaging in a septic patient with severe flank pain** – an obstructed kidney requires urgent decompression; antibiotics alone are ineffective.
- **Do NOT rely solely on urine dipstick** – negative nitrites do not rule out pyelo (false negatives common in enterococcal infections); always send a culture.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** High fever with rigors that does not settle, severe flank pain unresponsive to oral analgesia, inability to keep down fluids (vomiting), or dark/reduced urine output.
- **Follow-up:** If uncomplicated, no routine follow-up needed unless symptoms recur. If complicated (pregnancy, diabetes, CKD, recurrent), arrange GP review at 48–72 hours to check urine culture result, and consider outpatient renology/urology referral.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially if increasing despite fluids)
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L on repeat
  - Oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/hr for 2 hours)
  - Suspected obstruction (anuria, severe colicky pain)
  - No clinical response to IV antibiotics after 48 hours
- **Consider ICU referral if:**
  - Need for vasopressors (falling BP despite 30 mL/kg IV fluids)
  - GCS <15 or confusion
  - Respiratory rate >25 or SpO2 <92% on O2
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - Acute renal replacement therapy indicated (e.g. refractory hyperkalaemia, fluid overload, uraemia)
